title: Early Islam
description: Early Islam is a book by Desmond Stewart. This book cannot be imported into India.
descriptionParagraphs: Early Islam is a book by Desmond Stewart. This book cannot be imported into India.

What makes it interesting is that interpretation, devotion, satire, or doctrinal conflict becomes a matter of state administration. As a book, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in India. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: This book cannot be imported into India. The book purportedly contained grievous factual errors. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: Early Islam is a book by Desmond Stewart. This book cannot be imported into India.
banningParagraphs: Early Islam entered censorship debates as a book associated with doctrine, public controversy, and religion. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around religious control and religious offense.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1975 in India, where Government of India or British Indian authorities banned publication, sale, or possession. This book cannot be imported into India. The book purportedly contained grievous factual errors. This book cannot be imported into India. The book purportedly contained grievous factual errors.

counterReadings.title: Assassins of the Mind
counterReadings.author: Christopher Hitchens
counterReadings.note: Frames the Rushdie affair as a test of free speech against violent religious intimidation.

counterReadings.title: From Fatwa to Jihad
counterReadings.author: Kenan Malik
counterReadings.note: Tracks how conflicts over blasphemy, race, and offense evolved after the Rushdie controversy.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
